data.title: NatWest CEO Confident on Annual Cost Guide — Market Talk
data.description: NatWest is working to reduce its costs and drive multi-year efficiencies despite inevitable cost pressures like wage inflation, the U.K. bank's CEO says at a financial conference. NatWest is targeting roughly flat operating expenses at 8.1 billion pounds this year, including 100 million pounds in one-time charges for recent integrations. The lender has exited its Poland hub operation, has relocated a lot of its wealth business operations from Switzerland to the U.K. and India, has only renewed a portion of tech contracts, closed some branches and seen property consolidations, Paul Thwaite says. "The natural incentive we have is the more we can invest in the business to deliver--to create capacity--allows us to go even faster on driving those efficiencies out," he notes. Shares have gained 27% year to date. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

data.title: India's Inflation Softens to Lowest Reading Since 2019
data.description:
By Kimberley Kao

India's consumer price growth eased for a seventh consecutive month in May, supporting the central bank's decision to deliver a larger-than-expected rate cut at its most recent meeting.

The consumer price index rose 2.82% on the year in May, provisional government figures showed Thursday, down from 3.16% in April and lower than the 3.0% forecast by economists polled by The Wall Street Journal.

May's reading marks the softest inflation print since February 2019.

Economists had largely expected food and vegetable prices to remain subdued, keeping inflation below the Reserve Bank of India's optimal target of 4% and paving the way for further interest rate cuts to support the economy.

Food inflation, meanwhile, marked its lowest reading since October 2021.

Earlier this month, the Reserve Bank of India delivered a jumbo rate cut. Citing a marked pullback in inflation over the past six months, RBI Gov. Sanjay Malhotra said India's growth-inflation dynamics warranted frontloading policy easing to support growth.

The central bank also lowered its inflation forecast for the fiscal year to 3.7% from 4.0%.

"Growth, on the other hand, remains lower than our aspirations amidst [a] challenging global environment and heightened uncertainty," Malhotra said at the June policy meeting last week.

Nomura economists expect headline inflation to drop below 3.0% over the next six months, citing a strong winter crop harvest, easing pulse prices due to increased supply, lower agricultural input costs and potentially favorable monsoons.

data.title: Hooker Furnishings 1Q Loss Narrows, Sales Decline
data.description:
By Denny Jacob

Hooker Furnishings narrowed its quarterly loss even as sales declined, reflecting success in certain cost-cutting efforts.

The furnishings maker narrowed its loss to $3.05 million, or 29 cents a share, for the first quarter ended May 4, from $4.09 million, or 39 cents a share, in the prior-year period.

Sales declined to $85.3 million from $93.6 million.

Hooker attributed the overall decline in sales primarily to a double-digit sales decrease in the Home Meridian segment, which it says is positioned in the mid-price segment in which import tariffs have more sharply curtailed demand.

Chief Executive Jeremy Hoff said the company is making progress in areas it can control but adds that the industry continues to face a challenging environment due to persistent softness in the housing market, higher mortgage rates and declining consumer sentiment.

"In total, from the June 2024 start of our initiative, we anticipate reducing our total annual spend rate by approximately 25%" by next year, said Hoff, referring to cost-savings programs the company began last year.

The chief executive also said Hooker is waiting to hear in July what the final tariff may be for Vietnam, since the company sources about 80% of its products from there.

data.title: Boeing 787 Jet Bound for London Crashes in India With Hundreds on Board — WSJ
data.description:
By Shan Li

A London-bound Boeing 787 passenger jet carrying more than 200 people crashed near an airport in the western Indian city of Ahmedabad.

It was unclear if there were any casualties, according to an officer at the state police. The plane crashed shortly after taking off from the airport, the officer said.

The Air India flight departed Ahmedabad at 1:38 p.m. local time, the airline said in a statement. Of the 242 people on board, 169 were Indian nationals, 53 were British, one was Canadian and seven were Portuguese, the airline said.

Local television channels showed heavy plumes of smoke rising into the sky and emergency responders carrying people away from the scene on stretchers.

The plane, which was headed to London's Gatwick Airport, had reached an altitude of 625 feet when it stopped transmitting location data, according to Flightradar24. The plane is an 11-year-old Boeing 787, according to the flight-tracking service.

The plane crashed near a residential area of the city, according to an official of the state police.

"Shocked and devastated to learn about the flight crash in Ahmedabad," India's aviation minister, Kinjarapu Rammohan Naidu, wrote on X.

Shares in Boeing dropped sharply in premarket trading.

Write to Shan Li at shan.li@wsj.com
